body_txt: When the official history of the setting is overwritten by the ones in power and their Propaganda Machine . As such the real heroes and good guys are given Historical Villain Upgrade , the bad guys build a Cult of Personality around their actions and all the bad sides of the past is submitted to a Rose-Tinted Narrative . In Real Life and actual history, it is true that historical accounts can be subject to the personal bias of the writer, which is what the saying warns us about. However, applying the saying in a simplistic manner can potentially be as reductive, misleading, and more importantly just as binary as "the winners' narrative". To say that history is written by the winners should mean that we should treat both the winners and losers fairly and not present a one-sided view. Academic historians would qualify this by stating that most of our knowledge about our past is based only on a few sources, and furthermore, our understanding of history is never static and unchanging. History is, in truth, written and rewritten by all sorts of people with different agendas . There are a fair number of records biased in favor of the losers, as the survivors put pen to paper to gripe about how horrible the winners are, and how much better things would have been had they won . Or later enemies of the winners decide to give the past ones a better image. Also, oftentimes the "loser" of a conflict is not actually the group who suffered the most; consider the slaves in the American Civil War, or the Holocaust victims in WWII. The resolved conflict may well mark the end of some "losers"' troubles. Historiography is a discipline that actually studies and keeps track of how people's opinions about the past and certain events change throughout history. The reason for those changes is that people who win in one era can lose in the next, and their conquerors decide to write some new history books, while the old ones are still around. Sometimes the winners or their descendants even start feeling guilty, and allow a loser-slanted narrative to be taught in schools and such. Also worth pointing out that not every "winner" is going to be slick and sly about their crimes, simply because they don't view them as crimes . Many of history's "winners" were surprisingly honest about atrocities or even wrote proudly of how they brutally crushed their opponents, because it served their self-image and careerist aspirations. For example, Caesar wrote of his genocide of the Gauls in his personal memoirs , and historians doubt that account and other Roman accounts only because they feel Caesar is exaggerating, not only his actions but also those of his enemies, since Romans always liked painting their wars as "defensive" and their victories as underdog triumphs, so historians are skeptical about the moments when Romans invoke Worthy Opponent on their enemies. It likewise appealed to the vanity and pride of an Empire to preserve trophies and symbols of their conquest of foreign peoples, after all if the winner is writing a history, they need to brag about who they won over. In every work of propaganda there are, for lack of a better term, Freudian Slips and other moments that allow the multitude to peep over the shoulder of their rulers. Finally there is the obvious fact that even people on the same side will often disagree wildly on events and motivations . This is also connected with Grey-and-Gray Morality and Morality Kitchen Sink in that picking one side of the story in this context does not necessarily make it correct. After all, Your Terrorists Are Our Freedom Fighters . The use of this trope can be controversial with real-life history. People may feel there is a Writer on Board , or not take it seriously and think that Beethoven Was an Alien Spy is at play instead. So you'll often see the trope applied to completely fictional history . Such as the exploits of the national hero of a fictional country, or an Empire with a Dark Secret . The usual way is to provide a recap of the official version of events, have the hero find out the real and darker history, and start a conflict between him and the figures in power that want to keep it a secret. There are three possible outcomes. One, The Bad Guy Wins and official history stays. Two, the actual history comes to light, and Nothing Is the Same Anymore . Third, the hero realizes the scope of the changes that would take place and decides that it's not worth it; for good or evil, the history has already happened, and the truth would cause too much damage to those the hero cares about. A prime belief of every Conspiracy Theorist . Sub-trope of Might Makes Right . Supertrope of Internal Retcon , Pop-Cultural Osmosis and Hollywood History . Contrast You Cannot Kill an Idea and Won the War, Lost the Peace .
